title: Detect and repair literal \\n escape sequences in descriptions and comments
description:
AI coding agents frequently pass literal escape sequences like \n instead of real newlines when constructing CLI arguments. The text arrives looking like First linenSecond linenThird line — a single string with backslash-n characters. This story covers detecting and repairing that malformation, and emitting a warning that tells the caller exactly what happened and how to do it correctly.

The repair applies to both issue descriptions and comment bodies, on both create and update paths.

Scenario: Literal \n sequences are replaced with real newlines

Given an agent creates an issue with description SummarynnThis is the bodynWith multiple lines
When the CLI processes the description
Then the stored description contains real newline characters in place of each \n
And the issue is created successfully

Scenario: A warning is emitted when \n replacement occurs in a description

Given an agent creates an issue with description containing literal \n sequences
When the CLI processes the description
Then a warning is printed to stderr
And the warning message explains that \n escape sequences were replaced with real newlines
And the warning includes an example of how to pass a correctly formatted multi-line description using a heredoc or newline-containing string

Scenario: The same warning is emitted for comments

Given an agent posts a comment containing literal \n sequences
When the CLI processes the comment body
Then the \n sequences are replaced with real newlines
And a warning is printed to stderr explaining the transformation and the correct approach

Scenario: No warning is emitted for clean text

Given an agent creates an issue whose description contains real newlines and no literal \n sequences
When the CLI processes the description
Then no warning is printed and the text is stored as-is

Scenario: The warning message is machine-readable and actionable

Given the warning is emitted
Then it includes:
  - The word WARNING in the prefix
  - A brief statement that \n escape sequences were detected and replaced
  - A short example showing the correct shell invocation pattern (heredoc or $'...' syntax)
  - Enough context that an AI agent reading the warning can self-correct on the next call
creator: ryan.porter
